points: Health is defined by WHO as a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being—not merely the absence of disease. Physical well-being means a healthy body that functions properly; mental well-being includes positive emotions and emotional health; social well-being involves healthy relationships and community connections.

Staying healthy requires adopting good habits: eating balanced nutritious diet, exercising regularly, maintaining personal hygiene, getting adequate sleep, managing stress, and avoiding harmful substances like tobacco and alcohol. A clean environment with pure water and clean air is equally essential. These habits support both body and mind, and when they become daily routines, they lead to long-term health.

Health also depends on recognizing illness through symptoms (what we feel internally) and signs (observable changes like fever or rash). Mental and emotional well-being are crucial; loneliness, stress, and worry can make us sick even if we eat well and live cleanly. Strong relationships and emotional support are vital for overall health.

Communicable diseases are caused by pathogens (bacteria, viruses, fungi, worms, protozoa) that spread from infected to healthy persons through airborne transmission (coughing/sneezing), direct contact, contaminated food/water, or insect vectors like mosquitoes. Examples include cold, flu, chickenpox, measles, tuberculosis, hepatitis A, cholera, typhoid, malaria, and dengue. Prevention involves hygiene, handwashing, clean water/food, vaccination, and avoiding contact with infected persons.

Non-communicable diseases like diabetes, heart disease, cancer, asthma, and obesity result from unhealthy lifestyle, poor diet, lack of exercise, stress, and environmental factors—not pathogens. They cannot spread between people but have become India's leading cause of death. Deficiency diseases (scurvy, anaemia, goitre) result from nutritional lack. Prevention through lifestyle changes is more effective than treatment after disease develops.

Understanding disease types and transmission helps us take preventive action: handwashing reduces infection by 50%; clean sanitation significantly reduces diarrheal diseases; avoiding junk food and exercising regularly prevent lifestyle diseases. Community initiatives like improved sanitation have dramatically reduced communicable disease spread, particularly in children.

The immune system is the body's natural defense against pathogens. Immunity is the resistance to diseases—natural immunity is inborn resistance, while acquired immunity develops after exposure to a pathogen or vaccine. Vaccines train the immune system using weakened, dead, or harmless pathogen parts, providing protection without causing disease. Vaccines are preventive (prevent illness before it occurs) and are among the most effective public health tools, saving millions of lives yearly.

Antibiotics are medicines that kill bacteria and treat bacterial infections (discovered in 1928 when penicillin was found). However, antibiotics do not work against viral infections or diseases caused by protozoa—they specifically target bacterial cells. Indiscriminate antibiotic use has led to antibiotic resistance where bacteria survive treatment, making infections harder to treat. To preserve effectiveness, use antibiotics only when prescribed by doctors, in correct doses, for appropriate duration.

Disease treatment varies by type: communicable diseases caused by bacteria are treated with antibiotics; viral infections require supportive care allowing immune system to fight the virus. Non-communicable diseases require long-term management through medication, lifestyle changes, and regular care. Early diagnosis and continuous treatment are essential. India is a leading vaccine producer, playing crucial roles in global health initiatives including COVID-19 pandemic response.
